chunk_summary: Obesity in South Asian children is a chronic condition with higher cardiometabolic risks at lower body sizes, requiring proactive, longitudinal care tailored to India-specific phenotypes and BMI cutoffs.
learning_objectives: Understand obesity as a chronic disease influenced by biological, social, and environmental factors.
Recognize India-specific obesity risks, including higher visceral fat and cardiometabolic complications in South Asian children.
Learn the importance of early, longitudinal intervention to reduce cumulative health burdens.
Interpret India-specific BMI cutoffs and their implications for pediatric obesity management.
key_facts: Overweight prevalence in Indian children: 4% (ages 5–9) and 5% (adolescents) per CNNS (2016–2018).
NFHS-5 reports overweight prevalence in under-5 children at 3%–4%.
South Asian children exhibit higher visceral and ectopic fat for a given body mass compared to Western populations.
India-specific BMI cutoffs: 23 for overweight and 27 for obesity (adult-equivalent).
Obesity management requires longitudinal care with measurable goals and planned follow-ups.
reference_quote: South Asian children have higher cardiometabolic risk at lower body size, in part due to higher relative visceral and ectopic fat (especially liver) for a given body mass.
document_key_topics: Obesity as a chronic disease in pediatric practice
India-specific obesity prevalence and risk factors
South Asian phenotype and cardiometabolic risks
Longitudinal care strategies for obesity management
